Clean Vehicle Standard already has a significant impact on
our brands and the businesses that we represent, uh,
and on the customers that we serve.
Also, that impact will further in 2026
as tighter targets take effect
and the proposed amendments will compound it even more.
Our focus today is on ensuring the standard continues
to reduce emissions in a way that's fair, practical,
and sustainable for all key reefs.
The Clean Vehicle standard plays an important role in
reducing fleet CO2 emissions,
but it has to be fair, it has to be workable
and it has to encourage genuine
decarbonization over the long term.
It cannot distort the market
or load costs unevenly on certain kiwis
who have a genuine need for a specific vehicle.
At present, the Clean Vehicle Standard is struggling
to cut emissions as it was designed to, uh,
because it only regulates supply without a mechanism
to drive cus consumer demand,
the system cannot deliver the shift in CO2
that it was intended to.
As outlined in our submission, we support extending the life
of credits to four years.
This increases certainty and flexibility.
We also support extending borrowing provisions beyond 2025.
This gives importers the confidence to plan.
However, we remain opposed to opening credit transfers
between new and used importers, uh, at a two
for one exchange rate.
This has the potential to create arbitrage
and to undermined investment in lower emission vehicles
and fair competition.
It could also drive up the price
of credits making compliance harder
and more expensive for new importers and their customers.
We also remain strongly opposed to the removal
of weight adjusted targets
or the removal of the legislative requirement
to factor vehicle weight into CO2 targets.
Weight adjustment ensures fairness.
Small vehicles compared with small vehicles
and large vehicles with large vehicles
and targets are set based on the workload
capability of the vehicle.
Removing weight adjustment will result in significant cost
increase that will be passed on to consumers.
This will punish families
and businesses who genuinely need larger vehicles while
simultaneously stripping away credits from EVs.
Now to get a little technical, the positive correlation
between CO2
and vehicle weight known as the slope of the line will shift
to negative at relatively low EV uptake.
But true balance, when EV credits can offset debits doesn't
occur until EVs make up a far higher percentage of sales.
Removing weight adjustment at the slope
of the line flip point would therefore punish families
and businesses for years
before consumer demand for EVs enough is enough
to be give genuine balance.
So in closing, we support flexibility measures
that make the system more workable,
providing a clear pathway to carbon reduction.
We strongly urge the committee
to retain weight adjusted targets to preserve fairness
and credibility, to reconsider credit transfers between new
and used to prevent the possibility
of market gaming and distortion.
And to consider implementing appropriate
demand-based mechanisms.
To balance the clean vehicle standard,
we need a clean vehicle standard
that actually reduces emissions, not one
that distorts the market, punishes families
and incentivizes the retention
of older higher emission vehicles.
